title: 第六講
en: fTlHE genealogical classification of the Aryan lanJL guages was founded, as we saw, on a close comparison of the grammatical characteristics of each ; and it is the object of such works as Bopp's Comparative Grammar to show that the grammatical articulation of Sanskrit, Zend, Greek, Roman, Celtic, Teutonic, and Slavonic, was produced once and for all ; and that the apparent differences in the terminations of Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin, must be explained by laws of phonetic decay, peculiar to each dialect, which modified the original common Aryan type-, and changed it into so many national languages. It might seem, therefore, as if the object of comparative grammar was attained as soon as the exact genealogical relationship of languages had been settled ; and those who only looked to the higher problems of the science of language have not hesitated to declare that 'there is no painsworthy difficulty nor dispute about declension, number, case, and gender of nouns.' But although it is certainly true that comparative grammar is only a means, and that it has wellnigh taught us all that it has to teach — at least in the Aryan family of speech — it is to be hoped that, in the science of language, it will always retain that ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS. 223 prominent place which it has obtained through the labours of Bopp, Grimm, Pott, Benfey, Curtius, Kuhn, and others. Besides, comparative grammar has more to do than simply to compare. It would be easy enough to place side by side the paradigms of declension and conjugation in Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, and the other Aryan dialects, and to mark both their coincidences and their differences. But after we have done this, and after we have explained the phonetic laws which cause the primitive Aryan type to assume that national variety which we admire in Sanskrit, Greek, and Latin, new problems arise of a more interesting nature. We know that grammatical terminations, as they are now called, were originally independent words, and had their own purpose and meaning. Is it possible, after comparative grammar has established the original forms of the Aryan terminations, to trace them back to independent words, and to discover their original purpose and meaning? You will remember that this was the point from which we started. We wanted to know why the termination d in / loved should change a present into a past act. We saw that before answering this question we had to discover the most original form of this termination by tracing it from English to Gothic, and afterwards, if necessary, from Gothic, to Sanskrit. We return now to our original question, namely, What is language that a mere formal change, such as that of / love into / loved, should produce so very material a difference ?

Let us clearly see what we mean if we make a distinction between the radical and formal elements of a language ; and by formal elements I mean not only the terminations of declension and conjugation, 224 ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS.

but all derivative elements ; all, in fact, that is not radical. Our view on the origin of language must chiefly depend on the view which we take of these formal, as opposed to the radical, elements of speech. Those who consider that language is a conventional production, base their arguments principally on these formal elements. The inflections of words, they maintain, are the best proof that language was made by mutual agreement. They look upon them as mere letters or syllables without any meaning by themselves ; and if they were asked why the mere addition of a d changes / love into / loved, or why the addition of the syllable rai gave to faime, I love, the power of a future, fawner at, they would answer, that it was so because, at a very early time in the history of the world, certain persons, or families, or clans, agreed that it should be so.

This view was opposed by another which represents language as an organic and almost a living being, and explains its formal elements as produced by a principle of growth inherent in its very nature. 1 Languages,' * it is maintained, * are formed by a process, not of crystalline accretion, but of germinal development. Every essential part of language existed as completely (although only implicitly) in the primitive germ, as the petals of a flower exist in the bud before the mingled influences of the sun and the air caused it to unfold.' This view was first propounded by Frederick Schlegel,f and it is still held f ' It has been common among grammarians to regard those terminational changes as evolved by some unknown process from the body of a noun, as the branches of a tree spring from the stem — or as elements, unmeaning in themselves, but employed ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS. , 'J25 by many with whom poetical phraseology takes the place of sound and severe reasoning.

The science of language adopts neither of these views. As to imagining a congress for settling the proper exponents of such relations as nominative, genitive, singular, plural, active, and passive, it stands to reason that if such abstruse problems could have been discussed in a language void of inflections, there was no inducement for agreeing on a more perfect means of communication. And as to imagining language, that is to say, nouns and verbs, endowed with an inward principle of growth, all we can say is, that such a conception is really inconceivable. Language may be conceived as a production, but it cannot be conceived as a substance that could itself arbitrarily or conventionally to modify the meanings of words. This latter view is countenanced by Schlegel. " Languages with inflexions," says Schlegel, " are organic languages because they include a living principle of development and increase, and alone possess, if I may so express myself, a fruitful and abundant vegetation. The wonderful mechanism of these languages consists in forming an immense variety of words, and in marking the connection of ideas expressed by these words by the help of an inconsiderable number of syllables, which, viewed separately, have no signification, but which determine with precision the sense of the words to which they are attached. By modifying radical letters and by adding derivative syllables to the roots, derivative words of various sorts are formed, and derivatives from those derivatives. Words are compounded from several roots to express complex ideas. Finally, substantives, adjectives, and pronouns are declined, with gender, number, and case ; verbs are conjugated throughout voices, moods, tenses, numbers, and person*, by employing, in like manner, terminations and sometimes augments, which by themselves signify nothing. This method is attended with the advantage of enunciating in a single word the principal idea, frequently greatly modified, and extremely complex already, with its whole array of accessory ideas and mutable relations." ' — Transactions of the Philological Society, vol. ii.p. 39.

produce. But the science of language has nothing to do with mere theories, whether conceivable or not. It collects facts, and its only object is to account for these facts, as far as possible. Instead of looking on inflections in general either as conventional signs or natural excrescences, it takes each termination by itself, establishes its most primitive form by means of comparison, and then treats that primitive syllable as it would treat any other part of language — namely, as something which was originally intended to convey a meaning. Whether we are still able to discover the original intention of every part of language is quite a different question, and it should be admitted at once, that many grammatical forms, after they have been restored to their most primitive type, are still without an explanation. But with every year new discoveries are made by means of careful inductive reasoning. We become more familiar eveiy day with the secret ways of language, and there is no reason to doubt that in the end grammatical analysis will be as successful as chemical analysis. Grammar, though sometimes very bewildering to us in its later stages, is originally a much less formidable undertaking than is commonly supposed. What is grammar after all but declension and conjugation? Originally declension could not have been anything but the composition of a noun with some other word expressive of number and case. How the number was expressed, we saw in a former lecture. A very similar process led to the formation of cases.

Thus the locative is formed in various ways in Chinese:* one is by adding such words as cung, the OKIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS. 227 middle, or nei, inside. Thus, Mo-cung, in the empire, i sui ung, within a year. The instrumental is formed by the preposition y, which preposition is an old root, meaning to use. Thus, y ting, with a stick, where in Latin we should use the ablative, in Greek the dative. Now, however complicated the declensions, regular and irregular, may be in Greek and Latin, we may be certain that originally they were formed by this simple method of composition.

There was originally in all the Aryan languages a case expressive of locality, which grammarians call the locative. In Sanskrit every substantive has its locative, as well as its genitive, dative, and accusative. Thus, heart in Sanskrit is hrid ; in the heart, is hridi. Here, therefore, the termination of the locative is simply short i. This short i is a demonstrative root, and in all probability the same root which in Latin produced the preposition in. The Sanskrit hridi represents, therefore, an original compound, as it were, heart-within, which gradually became settled as one of the recognised cases of nouns ending in consonants. If we look to Chinese,* we find that the locative is expressed there in the same manner, but with a greater freedom in the choice of the words expressive of locality. c In the empire,' is expressed by Mo-cung ; ' within a year,' is expressed by i stii cung. Instead of cung, however, we might have employed other terms, such, for instance, as nei, inside. It might be said that the formation of so primitive a case as the locative offers little difficulty, but that this process of composition fails to account for the origin of the more abstract cases, the accusative, Q 2 228 ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS.

the dative, and the genitive. If we derive our notions of the cases from philosophical grammar, it is true, no doubt, that it would be difficult to realise by a simple composition the abstract relations supposed to be expressed by the terminations of the genitive, dative, and accusative. But remember that these are only general categories under which philosophers and grammarians have endeavoured to arrange the facts of language. The people with whom language grew up knew nothing of datives and accusatives. Everything that is abstract in language was originally concrete. If people wanted to say the King of Rome, they meant really the King at Rome, and they would readily have used what I have just described as the locative ; whereas the more abstract idea of the genitive would never enter into their system of thought. But more than this, it can be proved that the locative has actually taken, in some cases, the place of the genitive. In Latin, for instance, the old genitive of nouns in a was as. This we find still in pater familias, instead of pater familial or pater familice. The Umbrian and Oscan dialects retained the s throughout as the sign of the genitive after nouns in a. The ce of the Latin genitive, however, was originally aij that is to say, the old locative in i. ' King of Rome,' if rendered by Rex Romce, meant really ' King at Rome.' And here you will see how grammar, which ought to be the most logical of all sciences, is frequently the most illogical. A boy is taught at school, that if he wants to say ' I am staying at Rome,' he must use the genitive to express the locative. How a logician or grammarian can so twist and turn the meaning of the genitive as to make it express rest in a place, it is not for us to inquire; but, if he succeeded, his pupil would at ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS. 229 once use the genitive of Carthage (Carthaginis) or of Athens (Athenarum) for the same purpose, and he would then have to be told that these genitives could not be used in the same manner as the genitive of nouns in a. How all this is achieved by what is called philosophical grammar, we know not ; but comparative grammar at once removes all difficulty. It is only in the first declension that the locative has supplanted the genitive, whereas Carthaginis and Athenarum, being real genitives, could never be employed to express a locative. A special case, such as the locative, may be generalised into the more general genitive, but not vice versa.

You see thus by one instance how what grammarians call a genitive was formed by the same process of composition which we can watch in Chinese, and which we can prove to have taken place in the original language of the Aryans. And the same applies to the dative. If a boy is told that the dative expresses a relation of one object to another, less direct than that of the accusative, he may well wonder how such a flying arch could ever have been built up with the scanty materials which language has at her disposal ; but he will be still more surprised if, after having realised this grammatical abstraction, he is told that in Greek, in order to convey the very definite idea of being in a place, he has to use after certain nouns the termination of the dative. ' I am staying at Salarnis,' must be expressed by the dative Salammi. If you ask why? comparative grammar again can alone give an answer. The termination of the Greek dative in i was originally the termination of the locative. The locative may well convey the meaning of the dative, but the faded features of the 230 ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS.

dative can never express the freshness and distinctness of the locative. The dative Salamini was first a locative. ' I live at Salamis,' never conveyed the meaning, ' I live to Salamis.' On the contrary, the dative, in such phrases as ' I give it to the father,' was originally a locative ; and after expressing at first the palpable relation of ' I give it unto the father,' or ' I place it on or in the father,' it gradually assumed the more general, and less local, less coloured aspect which logicians and grammarians ascribe to their datives.* If the explanation just given of some of the cases in Greek and Latin should seem too artificial or too forced, we have only to think of French in order to see exactly the same process repeated under our eyes. The most abstract relations of the genitive, as, for instance, ' The immortality of the soul ' (Vimmortalite de rdme) ; or of the dative, as, for instance, ' I trust myself to God ' (je me fie a Dieu}, are expressed by prepositions, such as de and ad, which in Latin had the distinct local meanings of ' down from ' and 4 towards.' Nay, the English of and to, which have taken the place of the German terminations s and m, are likewise prepositions of an originally local character. The only difference between our erases and those of the ancient languages consists in this, — that the determining element is now placed before the word, whereas, in the original language of the Aryans, it was placed at the end.
What applies to the cases of nouns, applies with equal truth to the terminations of verbs. It may locative.' — Du Ponceau, p. 158.

ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS. 231 seem difficult to discover in the personal terminations of Greek and Latin the exact pronouns which were added to a verbal base in order to express / love, thou lovest, he loves ; but it stands to reason that originally these terminations must have been the same in all languages — namely, personal pronouns. We may be puzzled by the terminations of thou lovest and he loves, where st and s can hardly be identified with the modern thou and he-, but we have only to place all the Aryan dialects together, and we shall see at once that they point back to an original set of terminations which can easily be brought to tell their own story.

Let us begin with modern formations, because we have here more daylight for watching the intricate and sometimes wayward movements of language ; or better still, let us begin with an imaginary case, or with what may be called the language of the future, in order to see quite clearly how what we should call grammatical forms may arise. Let us suppose that the slaves in America were to rise against their masters, and, after gaining some victories, were to sail back in large numbers to some part of Central Africa, beyond the reach of their white enemies or friends. Let us suppose these men availing themselves of the lessons they had learnt in their captivity, and gradually working out a civilisation of their own. It is quite possible that, some centuries hence, a new Livingstone might find among the descendants of the American slaves, a language, a literature, laws, and manners, bearing a striking similitude to those of his own country. What an interesting problem for any future historian and ethnologist ! Yet there are problems in the past history 232 ORIGIN OF GRAMMATICAL FORMS.

of the world of equal interest, which have been and are still to be solved by the student of language. Now I believe that a careful examination of the language of the descendants of those escaped slaves would suffice to determine with perfect certainty their past history, even though no documents and no tradition had preserved the story of their captivity and liberation. At first, no doubt, the threads might seem hopelessly entangled. A missionary might surprise the scholars of Europe by an account of that new African language. He might describe it at first as very imperfect — as a language, for instance, so poor that the same word had to be used to express the most heterogeneous ideas. He might point out how the same sound, without any change of accent, meant true, a ceremony, a workman, and was used also as a verb in the sense of literary composition. All these, he might say, are expressed in that strange dialect by the sound rait (right, rite, wright, write). He might likewise observe that this dialect, as poor almost as Chinese, had hardly any grammatical inflections, and that it had no genders, except in a few words such as man-of-war and a railway-engine, which were both conceived as feminine beings, and spoken of as she. He might then mention an even more extraordinary feature, namely, that although this language had no terminations for the masculine and feminine genders of nouns, it employed a masculine and feminine termination after the affirmative particle, according as it was addressed to a lady or a gentleman. Their affirmative particle being the same as the English, Yes, they added a final r to it if addressed to a man, and a final m if addressed to a lady : that is to say, instead of simply saying Yes, these descen- YES R AND YES M. 233 dants of the escaped American slaves said Yesr to a man, and Yesm to a lady.

Absurd as this may sound, I can assure you that the descriptions which are given of the dialects of savage tribes, as explained for the first time by travellers or missionaries, are even more extraordinary. But let us consider now what the student of language would have to do, if such forms as Yes'r and Yes'm were, for the first time, brought under his notice. He would first have to trace them back historically, as far as possible, to their more original types, and if he discovered their connection with Yes Sir and Yes Mdm, he would point out how such contractions were most likely to spring up in a vulgar dialect. After having traced back the Yesr and Yesm of the free African negroes to the idiom of their former American masters, the etymologist would next inquire how such phrases as Yes Sir and Yes Madam came to be used on the American continent.

Finding nothing analogous in the dialects of the aboriginal inhabitants of America, he would be led, by a mere comparison of words, to the languages of Europe, and here again, first to the language of England. Even if no historical documents had been preserved, the documents of language would show that the white masters, whose language the ancestors of the free Africans adopted during their servitude, came originally from England, and, within certain limits, it would even be possible to fix the time when the English language was first transplanted to America. That language must have passed at least the age of Chaucer before it migrated to the New World. For Chaucer has two affirmative particles, Yea and Figs, and he distinguishes between the two.

He uses Yes only in answer to negative questions. For instance, in answer to ' Does he not go ? ' he would say Yes. In all other cases Chaucer uses Yea. To a question, ' Does he go ? ' he would answer Yea. He observes the same distinction between No and Nay, the former being used after negative, the latter after all other questions. This distinction became obsolete soon after Sir Thomas More,* and it must have become obsolete before phrases such as Yes Sir and Yes Madam could have assumed their stereotyped character.

But there is still more historical information to be gained from these phrases. The word Yes is Anglo-Saxon, the same as the German Ja, and it therefore reveals the fact that the white masters of the American slaves who crossed the Atlantic after the time of Chaucer, had crossed the Channel at an earlier period after leaving the continental fatherland of the Angles and Saxons. The words Sir and Madam tell us still more. They are Norman words, and they could only have been imposed on the Anglo-Saxons of Britain by Norman conquerors. They tell us more than this. For these Normans or Northmen spoke originally a Teutonic dialect, closely allied to Anglo-Saxon, and in that dialect words such as Sir and Madam could never have sprung up. We may conclude, therefore, that, previous to the Norman conquest, the Teutonic Northmen must have made a sufficiently long stay in one of the Roman provinces to forget their own and adopt the language of the Roman provincials.

We may now trace back the Norman Madam to YES R AND YES M. 235 the French Madame, and we recognise in this a corruption of the Latin Mea domina, my mistress. Domina was changed into domna, donna, and dame, and the same word Dame was also used as a masculine in the sense of lord, as a corruption of Domino, Domno, and Donno. The temporal lord ruling as ecclesiastical seigneur under the bishop, was called a vidame, as the Vidame of Chartres, &c. The French interjection Dame! has no connection with a similar exclamation in English, but it simply means Lord ! Dame-Dieu in old French is Lord God.* A derivative of Domina, mistress, was dominicella, which became Demoiselle and Damsel. The masculine Dame for Domino, Lord, was afterwards replaced by the Latin Senior, a translation of the German elder. This word elder was a title of honour, and we have it still both in alderman, and in what is originally the same, the English earl (the Norse Jarl), a comparative analogous to the A.-S. ealdor. This title Senior, meaning originally older, was but rarely f applied to ladies as a title of honour. Senior was changed into Seigneur, Seigneur into Sieur, and Sieur soon dwindled down to Sir.

Thus we see how in two short phrases, such as Yesr and Yesm, long chapters of history might be ' Ja dame Dieus non vuelha Qu'en ma colpa sia'l departimens.' (Que jamais le Seigneur Dieu ne veuille Qu'en ma faute soit la separation.)

read. If a general destruction of books, such as took place in China under the Emperor Thsin-chihoang-ti (213 B.C.), should sweep away all historical documents, language, even in its most depraved state, would preserve the secrets of the past, and would tell future generations of the home and migrations of their ancestors from the East to the West Indies.

It may seem startling at first to find the same name, the East Indies and the West Indies, at the two extremities of the Ayran migrations; but these very names are full of historical meaning. They tell us how the Teutonic race, the most vigorous and enterprising of all the members of the Aryan family, gave the name of West Indies to the country which, in their worldcompas^ing migrations, they imagined to be India itself; how they discovered their mistake, and then distinguished between the East Indies and West Indies ; how they planted new states in the west, and regenerated the effete kingdoms in the east ; how they preached Christianity, and at last practised it by abolishing slavery of body and mind among the slaves of West Indian landholders, and the slaves of Brahmanical soulholders, until they greeted at last the very homes from which the Aryan family had started when setting out on their discovery of the world. All this, and even more, may be read in the vast archives of language. The very name of India has a story to tell, for India is not a native name. We have it from the Romans, the Romans from the Greeks, the Greeks from the Persians. And why from the Persians ? Because it is only in Persian that an initial s is changed into 7t, which initial h was as usual dropped in Greek. It is only in Persian that the country of the Sindhu (sindhu is the Sanskrit J'AIMERAI. 237 name for river}, or of the seven sindhus, could have been called Hindia or India instead of Sindia. Unless the followers of Zoroaster had pronounced every s like A, we should never have heard of the West Indies !

We have thus seen by an imaginary instance what we must be prepared for in the growth of language, and we shall now better understand why it must be laid down as a fundamental principle in Comparative Grammar to look upon nothing in language as merely formal, till every attempt has been made to trace the formal elements of language back to their original and substantial prototypes. We are accustomed to the idea of grammatical terminations modifying the meaning of words. But words can be modified by words only ; and though in the present state of our science it would be too much to say that all grammatical terminations have been traced back to original independent words, so many of them have, even in cases where only a single letter was left, that we may well lay it down as a rule that all formal elements of language were originally substantial. Suppose English had never been written down before the time of Piers Ploughman. What should we make of such a form as nadistou* instead of ne hadst thou ? Ne rechi, instead of / reck not ? Al ffm in Dorsetshire is all of them. I midden is / may not ; / cooden, I could not. Yet the changes which Sanskrit had undergone before it was reduced to writing, must have been more considerable by far than what we see in these dialects, f f In Anglo-Saxon we find not for ne wot, I do not know; nist for he did not know; nisten for they did not know; nolde, 238 J AIMERAI.

Let us now look to modern classical languages such as French and Italian. Most of the grammatical terminations are the same as in Latin, only changed by phonetic corruption. Thus faime is ego amo ; tu aimes, tu amas ; il dime, ille amat. There was originally a final t in French il aime, and it comes out again in such phrases as aime-t-il? Thus the French imperfect corresponds to the Latin imperfect, the Parfait defini to the Latin perfect. But what about the French future? There is no similarity between amabo and f aimer ai. Here then we have a new grammatical form, sprung up, as it were, within the recollection of men ; or, at least, in the broad daylight of history. Now did the termination rai bud forth like a blossom in spring? or did some wise people meet together to invent this new termination, and pledge themselves to use it instead of the old termination bo ? Certainly not. We see first of all that in all the Romance languages the terminations of the future are identical with the auxiliary verb to have* In French you find —

j'ai and je chanter-ai nous avons and nous chanterons tu as tu chantcr-as vous avez vous chanterez il a il chanter-a ils ont ils chanteront But besides this, we actually find in Spanish and Provencal the apparent termination of the future used as an independent word and not yet joined to the infinitive. We find in Spanish, instead of ' lo hare,' I shall do it, the more primitive form hacer lo he, i.e.
noldest, for I would not, thou wouldst not ; nyle for I will not ; naebbe for I have not ; naefth for he has not ; naeron for they were not, &c.

J AIMEKAI. 239 facere id , habeo. We find in Proven£al dir vos ai instead of je vous dirai; dir vos em instead of nous vous dirons. There can be no doubt, therefore, that the Romance future was originally a compound of the auxiliary verb to have with an infinitive ; and / have to say easily took the meaning of / shall say.

Here, then, we see clearly how grammatical forms arise. A Frenchman looks upon his futures as merely grammatical forms. He has no idea, unless he is a scholar, that the terminations of his futures are identical with the auxiliary verb avoir. The Roman had no suspicion that amabo was a compound ; but it can be proved to contain an auxiliary verb as clearly as the French future. The Latin future was destroyed by means of phonetic corruption. When the final letters lost their distinct pronunciation, it became impossible to keep the imperfect amabam separate from the future amabo. The future was then replaced by dialectical regeneration, for the use of habeo with an infinitive is found in Latin, in such expressions as habeo dicere, I have to say, which would imperceptibly glide into I shall say.* In fact, wherever we look, we see that the future is expressed by means of composition. We have in English / shall and thou wilt, which mean originally / am bound and thou intendest. In German we use werden, the Gothic vairthan, which means originally to go, to turn towards. In modern Greek we find thelo, I will, in thelo dosei, I shall give. In Roumansch we meet with vegnir, to come, forming the future veng a vegnir, I shall come ; whereas iri French je viens de dire, I come from saying, is equivalent to 'I have 240 I LOVED.

just said.' The French ^'0 vais dire is almost a future, though originally it is vado dicere, I go to say. The Dorsetshire, * I be gwain to goo a-picken stuones,' is another case in point. Nor is there any doubt that in the Latin bo of amabo we have the old auxiliary bhii, to become, and in the Greek future in o-a>, the old auxiliary as, to be.* We now go back another step, and ask the question which we asked many times before, How can a mere d produce so momentous a change as that from / love to / loved? As we have learnt in the meantime that English goes back to Anglo-Saxon, and is closely related to continental Saxon and Gothic, we look at once to the Gothic imperfect in order to see whether it has preserved any traces of the original compound ; for, after what we have seen in the previous cases, we are no doubt prepared to find here, too, gramas an auxiliary verb to form certain futures in Greek. It has various meanings, but they can all be traced back to the Sanskrit man (manyate), to think. As anya, other, is changed to uXXoe, so manye, I think, to plXXta. Thus ptXXb> was used of things that were likely to be, as if these things themselves meant or intended to be or not to be; and, the original meaning being forgotten, /ne'XXw came to be a mere auxiliary expressing probability. Me'XXw and ptXXofiat, in the sense of ' to hesitate,' are equally explained by the Sanskrit man, to think or consider. In Old Norse the future is likewise formed by mttn, to mean.

In Gothic there is a verb nasjan, to nourish. Its preterite is as follows : —

ner-e-de ner-e-don ner-e-de ner-e-doa ner-e-de ner-e-don Let us now look to the auxiliary verb to do, in Anglo-Saxon.

Singular Plural dide didon didest didon dide didon If we had only the Anglo-Saxon preterite nerede and the Anglo-Saxon dide, the identity of the de in nerede with dide would not be very apparent. But here you will perceive the advantage which Gothic has over all other Teutonic dialects for the purposes of grammatical comparison and analysis. It is in Gothic, and in Gothic in the plural only, that the full R 242 I LOVED.

auxiliary dedum, dedujp, dedun, has been preserved. In the Gothic singular nasida, nasides, nasida stand for nasideda, nasidedes, nasideda. The same contraction has taken place in Anglo-Saxon, not only in the singular but in the plural also. Yet, such is the similarity between Gothic and Anglo-Saxon that we cannot doubt their preterites having been formed on the same last. If there be any truth in inductive reasoning, there must have been an original AngloSaxon preterite* —

Singular Plural ner-e-dide ner-e-didon ner-e-didest ner-e-didon ner-e-dide ner-e-didon And as ner-e-dide dwindled down to nerede, so nerede would, in modern English, become nered. The d of the preterite, therefore, which changes / love into I loved is originally the auxiliary verb to do, and 1 loved is the same as / love did, or / did love. In English dialects, as, for instance, in the Dorset dialect, every preterite, if it expresses a lasting or repeated action, is formed by / did, f and a distinction is thus established between "e died eesterdae,' and 1 the vo'ke did die by scores ; ' though originally died is the same as die did.

It might be asked, however, very properly, how did itself, or the Anglo-Saxon dide, was formed, and how it received the meaning of a preterite. In dide the final de is not a termination, but it is the root, and the first syllable di is a reduplication of the root.

ARYAN GRAMMAR. 243 The fact being that all preterites of old, or, as they are called, strong verbs, were formed as in Greek and Sanskrit by means of reduplication, reduplication being one of the principal means by which roots were invested with a verbal character.* The root do in Anglo-Saxon is the same as the root the in tithemi in Greek, and the Sanskrit root dhd in dadhdmi. AngloSaxon dide would therefore correspond to Sanskrit dadhau, I placed. »

Now, in this manner, the whole, or nearly the whole, grammatical framework of the Aryan or IndoEuropean languages has been traced back to original independent words, and even the slightest changes which at first sight seem so mysterious, such as foot into feet, or / find into 1 found, have been fully accounted for. This is what is called comparative grammar, or a scientific analysis of all the formal elements of a language preceded by a comparison of all the varieties which one and the same form has assumed in the numerous dialects of the Aryan family. The most important dialects for this purpose are Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, and Gothic; but in many cases Zend, or Celtic, or Slavonic dialects come in to throw an unexpected light on forms unintelligible in any of the four principal dialects. The result of such a work as Bopp's Comparative Grammar of the Aryan languages may be summed up in a few words. The whole framework of grammar — the elements of derivation, declension, and conjugation — had become settled before the separation of the Aryan family. Hence the broad outlines of grammar, in Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Gothic, and the rest, are in reality the s 2 244 ARYAN CIVILISATION.

same ; and the apparent differences can be explained by phonetic corruption, which is determined by the phonetic peculiarities of each nation. On the whole, the history of all the Aryan languages is nothing but a gradual process of decay. After the grammatical terminations of all" these languages have been traced back to their most primitive form, it is possible, in many instances, to determine their original meaning. This, however, can be done by means of induction only ; and the period during which, as in the Proven9al dir vos ai, the component elements of the old Aryan grammar maintained a separate existence in the language and the mind of the Aryans, had closed before Sanskrit was Sanskrit or Greek Greek. That there was such a period we can doubt as little as we can doubt the real existence of fern forests previous to the formation of our coal fields. We can do even more. Suppose we had no remnants of Latin ; suppose the very existence of Rome and of Latin were unknown to us ; we might still prove, on the evidence of the six Romance dialects, that there must have been a time when these dialects formed the language of a small settlement ; nay, by collecting the words which all these dialects share in common, we might to a certain extent reconstruct the original language, and draw a sketch of the state of civilisation, as reflected by these common words. The same can be done if we compare Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Gothic, Celtic, and Slavonic. The words which have as nearly as possible the same form and meaning in all the languages must have existed before the people, who afterwards formed the prominent nationalities of the Aryan family, separated ; and, if carefully interpreted, they, too, will serve as evidence as to the state of ARYA. 245 civilisation attained by the Aryans before they left their common home. It can be proved by the evidence of language, that before their separation the Aryans led the life of agricultural nomads — a life such as Tacitus describes that of the ancient Germans. They knew the arts of ploughing, of making roads, of building ships, of weaving and sewing, of erecting houses j they had counted at least as far as one hundred. They had domesticated the most important animals, the cow, the horse, the sheep, the dog ; they were acquainted with the most useful metals, and armed with iron hatchets, whether for peaceful or warlike purposes. They had recognised the bonds of blood and the bonds of marriage ; they followed their leaders and kings, and the distinction between right and wrong was fixed by laws and customs. They were impressed with the idea of a Divine Being, and they invoked it by various names. All this, as I said, can be proved by the evidence of language. For if you find that languages like Greek, Latin, Gothic, Celtic, or Slavonic, which, after their first separation, have had but little contact with Sanskrit, have the same word, for instance, for iron which exists in Sanskrit, this is proof absolute that iron was known previous to the Aryan separation. Now, iron is ais in Gothic, and ayas in Sanskrit, a word which, as it could not have been borrowed by the Indians from the Germans or by the Germans from the Indians, must have existed previous to their separation. "We could not find the same name for house in Sanskrit, Greek, Latin, Slavonic, and Celtic,* unless houses had been known before the separation of these dialects. In this manner a history of Aryan civilisation has 246 ARYA.

been written from the archives of language, stretching back to times far beyond the reach of any documentary history.* The very name of Arya belongs to this history, and I shall devote the rest of this lecture to tracing the origin and gradual spreading of this old word. I had intended to include, in to-day's lecture, a short account of comparative mythology, a branch of our science which restores the original form and meaning of decayed words by the same means by which comparative grammar recovers the original form and meaning of terminations. But my time is too limited ; and, as I have been asked repeatedly why I applied the name of Aryan to that family of language which we have just examined, I feel that I am bound to give an answer.

Arya is a Sanskrit word, and in the later Sanskrit it means noble, of a good family. It was, however, originally a national name, and we see traces of it as late as the Law-book of the Manavas, where India is still called Arya-avarta, the abode of the Aryas.\ In the old Sanskrit, in the hymns of the Veda, drya occurs frequently as a national name and as a name of honour, comprising the worshippers of the gods of the Brahmans, as opposed to their enemies, who are called in the Veda Dasyus. Thus one of the gods, Indra, who, in some respects, answers to the Greek Zeus, is invoked in the following words (Rigarid they who are Dasyus ; punish the lawless, and .* See M. ARTA. 247 deliver them unto thy servant ! Be thou the mighty helper of the worshippers, and I will praise all these thy deeds at the festivals.'

In the later dogmatic literature of the Vedic age, the name of Arya is distinctly appropriated to the three first castes — the Brahmans, Kshatriyas, Vaisyas — as opposed to the fourth, or the Sudras. In the Satapatha-Bradhmana it is laid down distinctly : ' Aryas, are only the Brahmans, the Kshatriyas, and Vaisyas, for they are admitted to the sacrifices. They shall not speak with everybody, but only with the Brahman, the Kshatriya, and the Vaisya. If they should fall into a conversation with a Sudra, let them say to another man, " Tell this Sudra so." This is the law.'
In the Atharva-veda (iv. 20,4; xix. 62, 1) expressions occur such as, ' seeing all things, whether Sudra or Arya,' where Sudra and Arya are meant to express the whole of mankind.

This word drya with a long d is derived from arya with a short a, and this name arya is applied in the later Sanskrit to a Vaisya, or a member of the third caste.* What is called the third class must originally have constituted the large majority of the Brahmanic society, for all who were not soldiers or priests were Vaisyas. We may well understand, therefore, how a name, originally applied to the cultivators of the soil and householders, should in time have become the general name of all Aryans. f Why* f In one of the Vedas, arya with a short a is used like arya, as opposed to 6udra. For we read (Vaj-San. xx. 17): ' Whatever sin we have committed in the village, in the forest, in the home, in the open air, against a Sudra, against an Arya — thou art our deliverance.'

the householders were called arya is a question which would carry us too far at present. I can only state that the etymological signification of Arya seems to be, ' one who ploughs or tills,' and that it is connected with the root of arare. The Aryans would seem to have chosen this name for themselves as opposed to the nomadic races, the Turanians, whose original name Tura implies the swiftness of the horseman. ^ In India, as we saw, the name of Arya, as a national name, fell into oblivion in later times, and was preserved only in the term Aryavarta, the abode of the Aryans. But it was more faithfully preserved by the Zoroastrians who migrated from India to the north-west, and whose religion has been preserved to us in the Zend-avesta, though in fragments only. Now Airy a in Zend means venerable, and is at the same time the name of the people.* In the first chapter of the Vendiddd, where Ahuramazda explains to Zarathustra the order in which he created the earth, sixteen countries are mentioned, each, when created by Ahuramazda, being pure and perfect ; but each being tainted in turn by Angro mainyus or Ahriman. Now the first of these countries is called Airyanem vaejo, Arianum semen, the Aryan seed, and its position is supposed to have been as far east as the western slopes of the Belurtag and Mustag, near the sources of the Oxus and Y axartes, the highest elevation of Central Asia.f From this country, which is called their seed, the Aryans, according to their own traditions, advanced towards the south and west, and in the Zend-avesta the whole extent of country occupied by the Aryans is likewise called Airyd. Though the relation between Aryans and Turanians was hostile, and though there were continual wars between them, as we learn from the great Persian epic, the Shahnameh, it does not follow that all the nomad races who infested the settlements of the Aryans were of Tatar blood and speech. Turvasa and his descendants, who represent the Turanians, are described in the later epic poems of India as cursed and deprived of their inheritance in India; but in the Yedas Turvasa is represented as worshipping Aryan gods. Even in the Shahnameh, Persian heroes go over to the Turanians and lead them against Iran, very much as Coriolanus led the Samnites against Rome. We may thus understand why so many Turanian or Scythian names, mentioned by Greek writers, should show evident traces of Aryan origin. Aspa was the Persian name for horse, and in the Scythian names Aspabota, Aspakara, and Asparatlia* we can hardly fail to recognise the same element. Even the name of the Aspasian mountains, placed by Ptolemy in Scythia, indicates a similar origin. Nor is the word Arya unknown beyond the Oxus. There is a people called Ariacce^ another called Antariani."^ A king of the Scythians, at the time of Darius, was called Ariantes. A contemporary of Xerxes is known by the name of Aripithes (i. e. "We have thus traced the name of Arya from India to the west, from Aryavarta to Ariana, Persia, Media, more doubtfully to Armenia and Albania, to the Iron in the Caucasus, and to some of the nomad tribes in Transoxiana. As we approach Europe the traces of this name grow fainter, yet they are not altogether lost.

Two roads were opened to the Aiyans of Asia in their westward migrations. One through Chorasan * to the north, through what is now called Russia, and thence to the shores of the Black Sea and Thrace. Another from Armenia, across the Caucasus or across the Black Sea to Northern Greece, and along the Danube to Germany. Now on the former road the Aryans left a trace of their migrations in the old name of Thrace, which was Aria ;f on the latter we meet in the eastern part of Germany, near the Vistula, with a German tribe called Arii. And as in Persia we found many proper names in which Arya formed an important ingredient, so we find again in German history names such as Ariovistus."^ Though we look in vain for any traces of this old national name among the Greeks and Romans, some scholars believe that it may have been preserved in the extreme west of the Aryan migrations, in the veiy name of Ireland. The common etymology of Erin is that it means ' island of the west,' iar-innis, or land of of Darius.

ARYA. 257 to-day, and will probably have it for ever, which was given to it by the earliest Latin and Sabine settlers ; and wherever we find the name of Rome, whether in Wallachia, which by the inhabitants is called Rumania, or in the dialects of the Grisons, the Romansch, in the title of the Romance languages, or in the name of Rouma, given by the Arabs to the Greeks, and in that of Roumelia, we know that some threads would lead us back to the Rome of Romulus and Remus, the stronghold of the earliest warriors of Latium. The ruined city near the mouth of the Upper Zab, now usually known by the name of Nimrud, is called Athur by the Arabic geographers, and in Athur we recognise the old name of Assyria, which Dio Cassius writes Atyria, remarking that the barbarians changed the Sigma into Tau. Assyria is called Athura in the inscriptions of Darius.* We hear of battles fought on the SutLedge, and we hardly think that the battlefield of the Sikhs was nearly the same where Alexander fought the kings of the Penjab. But the name of the Sutledge is the name of the same river as the Hesudrus of Alexander, the Satadru of the Indians, and among the oldest hymns of the Yeda, about 1500 B.C., we find a war-song referring to a battle fought on the two banks of the same stream.

No doubt there is danger in trusting to mere similarity of names. Grimm may be right that the Arii of Tacitus were originally Harii, and that their name is not connected with Arya. But the evidence on either side being merely conjectural, this must remain an open question. In most cases, however, a strict observation of the phonetic laws peculiar to each language will remove all uncertainty. Grimm, S 258 AKYA.

Without denying the possibility of the correctness of this view, which is partially supported by the accusative vidoyum, from vidaevo, enemy of the Divs, there is no reason why Haroyum should not be taken for a regular accusative of ffaroyu, the long u in the accusative being due to the final nasal. (Burnouf, Yasna, Notes, p. 103.) This Haroyu would be in the nominative as regular a form as Sarayu in Sanskrit, nay even more regular, as haroyu would presuppose a Sanskrit sarasyu or saroyn, from saras.

AKYA. 259 yond the time of Zoroaster. The Zoroastrians were a colony from northern India. They had been together for a time with the people whose sacred songs have been preserved to us in the Veda. A schism took place, and the Zoroastrians migrated westward to Arachosia and Persia. In their migrations they did what the Greeks did when they founded new colonies, what the Americans did in founding new cities. They gave to the new cities and to the rivers along which they settled, the names of cities and rivers familiar to them, and reminding them of the localities which they had left. Now, as a Persian h points to a Sanskrit s, Haroyu would be in Sanskrit Saroyu. One of the sacred rivers of India, a river mentioned in the Veda, and famous in the epic poems as the river of Ayodhy&, one of the earliest capitals of India, the modern Awadh or Hanuman-garhi, has the name of Sarayu, the modern Sarju* As Comparative Philology has thus traced the ancient name of Arya from India to Europe, as the original title assumed by the Aryans before they left their common home, it is but natural that it should have been chosen as the technical term for the family of languages which was formerly designated as IndoGermanic, Indo-European, Caucasian, or Japhetic.
Sarayu occurs also with a long u; see Wilson, s. v. M. Oppert rightly identifies the people of Haraiva with the 'Aptlot, not, like Grimm, with the "Ap«ot.

saras, water, sarit, river, and Sarayu, the proper name of the river near the capital of Oude ; and we may conclude with great probability that this Sarayu or Sarasyu gave the name to the river Arius or Heri, and to the country of "Apia or Herat. zh: 雅利安語言的譜系分類是建立在對各語言文法特徵的密切比較之上的，正如我們所見；而博普《比較文法》這類著作的目標就是要說明：梵文、斯拉夫語、希臘文、羅馬文、凱爾特文、日耳曼文及斯拉夫語的文法結構是一次性地產生的；梵文、希臘文及拉丁文詞尾的表面差異必須透過各方言特有的音韻衰退規律來解釋，這些規律改變了原始共通的雅利安類型，並將其轉變成眾多民族語言。因此，似乎只要確定了語言的確切譜系關係，比較文法的目標就已達成；那些只關注語言科學更高層問題的人，不曾猶豫地宣稱「關於名詞的格系、數、格位和性別，實無值得費力的困難或爭議」。然而，儘管比較文法確實只是一種手段，且已大致傳授了它所有的內容——至少在雅利安語族中如此——但人們期望在語言科學中，它將永遠保持通過博普、格林、波特、本費、庫提烏斯、庫恩及其他人的努力所獲得的那一重要地位。此外，比較文法所做的不止於單純的比較。將梵文、希臘文、拉丁文及其他雅利安方言的格系及動詞變位表並排放置，並標出它們的相同和差異之處，這是輕而易舉的。但在我們完成了這項工作，並解釋了導致原始雅利安類型呈現出梵文、希臘文及拉丁文中令我們讚嘆的民族多樣性的音韻規律之後，新的、更有趣的問題就應運而生。我們知道現今所謂的文法詞尾原本是獨立的詞彙，擁有自己的目的和意義。在比較文法確立了雅利安詞尾的原始形式之後，是否有可能將它們追溯回獨立的詞彙，並發現它們的原初目的和意義呢？你會記得這正是我們開始的出發點。我們想知道為什麼*I loved*中的詞尾*d*應該將現在式轉變為過去式。我們看到在回答這個問題之前，必須通過從英文追溯到哥特文，然後如有必要從哥特文追溯到梵文，來發現這個詞尾的最原初形式。我們如今回到我們最初的問題，即：語言是什麼，竟使得*I love*轉變為*I loved*這樣的純粹形式改變，應該產生如此重大的實質差異？
讓我們清楚地看出，倘若我們區分語言的根本要素與形式要素，我們的意思為何；而我所謂的形式要素，不僅指涵蓋了詞格變系統與動詞變位系統的詞尾，

但所有的衍生要素；事實上，所有不屬於詞根的部分。我們對語言起源的看法在很大程度上取決於我們對這些形式要素（相對於詞根要素）的認識。那些認為語言是約定俗成產物的人，主要是基於這些形式要素來論證的。他們主張，詞的屈折變化是語言由共同協議制定的最佳證明。他們把這些屈折視為純粹的字母或音節，本身毫無意義；如果有人問他們為何單純加上 d 會把 love 變成 loved，或為何加上音節 rai 會賦予 faime（我愛）以未來時的力量，變成 fawner at（我將愛），他們會回答說，這是因為在世界歷史的極早時期，某些人、或某些家族、或某些氏族，彼此達成協議，決定應當如此。

這一觀點遭到另一種觀點的反對，後者將語言視為有機體，幾乎是一個活的生命，並將其形式要素解釋為源自其本質中內在的生長原理。«語言»，人們主張，«是通過一種過程形成的，不是結晶堆積的過程，而是胚胎發展的過程。語言的每一個本質部分在原始胚胎中都已完全存在（儘管只是隱含地），就如同花瓣在花蕾中存在一樣，只待陽光與空氣的混合影響使其綻放。» 這一觀點首先由弗里德里希‧施萊格爾提出，至今仍被許多人持有，他們以詩意的措辭取代了健全而嚴謹的推理。 文法形式的起源中，語法學家常見的做法是，將那些詞尾變化視為通過某個未知過程從名詞本體中演化而來，就像樹枝從樹幹上生長出來——或視為本身無意義的要素，但被採用來[...]。

語言科學既不採納上述任一觀點。關於想像一場集會來界定主格、屬格、單數、複數、主動態、被動態等此類關係的適當指數，理所當然的是，倘若在無屈折語言中能夠討論這些深奧的問題，就沒有必要同意採用更完善的溝通手段。至於想像語言——也就是名詞和動詞——稟受內在的成長原則，我們只能說這樣的觀念確實是不可思議的。語言可以被理解為一種產物，但它不能被理解為一種實體，足以任意地或約定俗成地改變詞義。這後一種觀點得到施萊格爾（Schlegel）的支持。施萊格爾說：「帶屈折的語言是有機語言，因為它們蘊含活的發展原則與增長原則，而且——如果我可以這樣表達——唯獨它們具有肥沃豐盛的植被。這些語言的奇妙機制在於形成數量龐大的詞彙，並通過幫助相對較少的音節——這些音節單獨觀看時毫無意義——來標示由這些詞所表達的觀念之連結，這些音節以精確的方式決定了所附著詞語的意義。通過改變詞根字母和向詞根添加衍生音節，形成各類衍生詞，以及衍生詞的衍生詞。由數個詞根組合而成的複合詞用以表達複雜觀念。最後，名詞、形容詞和代詞按性、數、格變化；動詞通過聲態、語氣、時態、人稱等全面進行結合，以同樣方式採用詞尾和有時增補字母，這些本身毫無意義。這一方法的優點在於以單一詞語表述主要觀念——常常已經大幅修飾、極其複雜——並帶有其全部附屬觀念與可變關係的陣容。」——《語文學會會刊》第二卷，第39頁。

語言科學對單純的理論——無論其是否可想像——毫不關涉。它蒐集事實，其唯一目的乃是盡可能地說明這些事實。語言科學不是籠統地將詞尾視為習慣性的符號或自然的贅疣，而是逐一檢視每個詞尾，通過比較法確立其最原始的形態，然後把那個原始音節視為語言的任何其他部分來處理——亦即，把它看作原本旨在傳達某種意義的東西。我們是否仍然能夠發現語言各部分的原始意圖，這是完全不同的問題；應該坦率地承認，許多文法形式在被恢復到其最原始的類型之後，仍然缺乏解釋。然而，隨著每一年的進展，通過謹慎的歸納推理，新的發現不斷湧現。我們每天都對語言的祕密機制愈發熟悉，而沒有理由懷疑最終文法分析將會如同化學分析一樣成功。文法儘管在其較後的階段有時令我們感到困惑，但在其原始形態中，是一項遠不如人們通常所認為的那麼艱鉅的事業。畢竟，文法不過是詞形變化和動詞變位而已？原本詞形變化不可能是任何東西，除了名詞與某些表達數和格的其他詞的複合。數是如何表達的，我們在前一場講座中已經看過。格的形成過程大致相同。

因此，位置格在中文裡以各種方式構成：*其一是藉由添加如 cung（中間）或 nei（內部）等詞彙。例如，Mo-cung，在帝國之中；i sui ung，一年之內。工具格則由前置詞 y 構成，該前置詞乃是古老詞根，意為使用。例如，y ting，用棍子，在拉丁文中我們應使用奪格，在希臘文中則使用與格。然而，無論希臘文與拉丁文的格變系統，無論規則或不規則，如何繁複，我們可以確定的是，它們最初都是以這種簡單的複合方法所構成的。

在所有雅利安語言中，原本都存在一個表示地點的格，文法家稱之為位置格。在梵文中，每一個名詞都有其位置格，就如同它具有屬格、與格和賓格一樣。因此，心臟在梵文中是 hrid；在心臟之中，則是 hridi。在此，位置格的詞尾僅僅是短母音 i。這個短母音 i 是一個指示根，並且極有可能是同一個根，在拉丁文中產生了前置詞 in。因此，梵文 hridi 代表了一個原始的複合形式，如同說「心臟－在內」（heart-within），其後逐漸確立為以輔音結尾的名詞之一個公認格。若我們考察漢語，我們會發現位置格在那裡用同樣的方式表達，但對於表示地點的詞的選擇卻具有更大的自由度。「在帝國之中」用 Mo-cung 表達；「在一年之內」用 i stii cung 表達。然而代替 cung，我們本可使用其他術語，例如 nei（內部）。有人或許會說，如此原始的格式如位置格的形成提供的困難不大，但這種複合的過程未能解釋更為抽象格式的起源，例如賓格。

格位詞、與屬格。若我們從哲學文法的角度推導格位的觀念，固然誠然有理，但若要透過簡單的複合方式來實現屬格、與格、及賓格的詞尾所應表達的抽象關係，便會顯得困難。然而須記取，這些不過是哲學家與文法家為了整理語言事實而試圖建立的一般性範疇。語言賴以成長的民族對與格與賓格一無所知。語言中一切抽象的元素原本皆是具體的。若人們想要表達「羅馬的國王」，他們實際上是指「在羅馬的國王」，他們很樂意使用我剛才所述的位格（locative）；而屬格那種更加抽象的觀念決不會進入他們的思維系統。不僅如此，甚且可以證明，在某些情況下，位格確實已取代了屬格的地位。例如在拉丁文中，古代 a 結尾名詞的屬格是 as。我們至今仍可在 pater familias 中看到這種形式，而非 pater familial 或 pater familice。翁布里亞（Umbrian）與奧斯干（Oscan）方言則始終保留 s 作為 a 結尾名詞屬格的標記。然則拉丁文屬格的 ce 本為 aij，亦即古代的位格 i。「羅馬的國王」若譯為 Rex Romae，實則意為「在羅馬的國王」。在此你會看到，原應是最具邏輯性的科學——文法——往往卻是最不合邏輯的。一個男童在學校裡被教導，若要表達「我停留在羅馬」，他必須使用屬格來表現位格。邏輯學家或文法家究竟如何能夠如此曲解屬格的意義，使其表達停留於某地，此非我輩所須深究；但假若他成功了，他的學生隨即會用迦太基的屬格（Carthaginis）或雅典的屬格（Athenarum）作同樣用途，而後他便不得不被告知，這些屬格不能以與 a 結尾名詞屬格相同的方式使用。這一切究竟是如何透過所謂「哲學文法」達成的，我們無從得知；但比較文法卻立刻消解了一切困難。位格唯獨在第一變系中才取代了屬格，反觀 Carthaginis 與 Athenarum 既為真正的屬格，便決不能用來表現位格。一種特殊的格位，如位格，可以被推廣為更為普遍的屬格，但反向之事則不然。

你可以透過這個例子看出，文法學家所謂的屬格是透過與我們在漢語中能夠觀察到的同一個合成過程而形成的，而且我們可以證明這個過程曾經發生在雅利安人的原始語言中。同樣的情況也適用於與格。如果一個男孩被告知與格表達的是兩個對象之間的關係，比起賓格的關係來得較不直接，他自然會驚異於這樣一道飛架怎麼可能用語言所擁有的如此貧瘠的材料來建造；但當他領悟了這個文法上的抽象概念之後，若被告知在希臘語中，為了表達「在某個地方逗留」這樣一個非常確定的觀念，他必須在某些名詞之後使用與格的詞尾時，他會更加驚訝。「我正在薩拉米斯逗留」必須用與格 Salamini 來表達。如果你問為什麼？比較文法學才能提供唯一的答案。希臘語與格以 i 結尾的詞尾原本是位置格（locative）的詞尾。位置格確實可以表達與格的意義，但這些已經褪色的特徵

與格永遠無法表達位置格的清新與鮮明。與格 Salamini 最初原是位置格。「我住在薩拉米斯」（I live at Salamis）絕未曾傳達「我住向薩拉米斯」（I live to Salamis）的意思。相反地，與格在「我把它給予父親」（I give it to the father）這類短語中，最初原是位置格；在首先表達了「我把它給予父親」或「我把它放在父親身上或父親之中」這種可感知的關係之後，它逐漸呈現出邏輯學家與文法家們所賦予與格的更為抽象、較少涉及位置、較少著色的樣貌。* 若前述對於希臘文與拉丁文某些格位所給予的解釋看似過於人為或過於生硬，我們只需想到法文便可眼見完全相同的過程正在我們眼前重演。屬格最為抽象的關係，例如「靈魂的不朽」（l'immortalité de l'âme）；或與格，例如「我將自己委託給上帝」（je me fie à Dieu），均由前置詞如 de 與 ad 來表達，而這些前置詞在拉丁文中具有「從……向下」與「朝向」的明確位置意義。更進一步言，取代了日耳曼文詞尾 s 與 m 的英文 of 與 to，同樣是原本具有位置特性的前置詞。我們當代語言與古代語言間的唯一差異在於——決定性的成分如今置於詞語之前，而在雅利安人的原始語言中，它則置於詞尾。
適用於名詞情況的原則，同樣適用於動詞詞尾。杜龐索（Du Ponceau）第一五八頁曾述及此事。

文法形式的起源。231 在希臘文和拉丁文的人稱詞尾中，似乎難以發現那些曾被添加到動詞詞根以表達「我愛」、「汝愛」、「他愛」的準確人稱代詞；但理所當然，這些詞尾在原始語言中必定在所有語言中相同——即人稱代詞。我們在「汝愛」和「他愛」的詞尾上可能感到困惑，其中 st 和 s 難以與現代的「汝」和「他」相對應；然而，我們只需將所有雅利安方言匯集在一起，便能立刻看出它們都指向一套原始詞尾，這套詞尾很容易被引導自我證明。

讓我們從現代語言的形成開始，因為在此我們有更多的光亮來觀察語言那些複雜且有時難以捉摸的運動；或者更確切地說，讓我們從一個假想的案例開始，或稱之為未來的語言，以便清楚地看到我們所謂的語法形式是如何產生的。讓我們假設美國的奴隸起來反抗他們的主人，並在取得某些勝利後，大批人馬駛回到中非的某個地區，遠離他們的白人敵人或友邦的控制。讓我們假設這些人利用他們在被囚禁期間所學到的教訓，逐漸建立起自己的文明。完全可能的是，在幾個世紀之後，某位未來的利文斯頓可能會在美國奴隸的後代中發現一種語言、一種文學、法律和風俗習慣，與他自己國家的相差無幾。這對於未來的任何歷史學家和民族學家來說，是多麼有趣的問題啊！然而，在過去的歷史中還存在著許多問題。

世界上還有許多同樣值得關注的問題，這些問題過去曾經、現在仍然需要語言科學的研究者去解決。現在我相信，只要仔細檢視那些逃脫的奴隸後裔所使用的語言，即使沒有任何文獻記載和傳統故事保存他們被奴役和獲得解放的歷史，我們也能夠以完全確定的方式判斷出他們的過去歷史。起初，毫無疑問，這些線索似乎會令人絕望地糾纏在一起。一位傳教士也許會以一種新的非洲語言的描述令歐洲的學者們感到驚訝。他起初也許會把它描述為非常不完善的語言——例如說，這種語言非常貧乏，以至於同一個詞必須被用來表達最不相同的概念。他也許會指出，同一個聲音在沒有任何重音變化的情況下，既表示真實，又表示儀式，還表示工匠，並且也被用作動詞，意思是文學創作。他也許會說，所有這些在那種奇異的方言中都由聲音 rait（right, rite, wright, write）來表達。他同樣也許會觀察到，這種方言幾乎和中文一樣貧乏，幾乎沒有任何文法屈折變化，並且沒有性數變化，除了少數幾個詞彙如 man-of-war 和 railway-engine，這兩者都被視為陰性存在物，被稱作 she。他也許接著提到一個更加非凡的特點，也就是說，雖然這種語言在名詞的陽性和陰性性數上沒有詞尾變化，但它在肯定詞之後使用陽性和陰性的詞尾變化，這根據所表達對象是一位女士還是一位紳士而定。他們的肯定詞與英文的 Yes 相同，但如果是針對男性就在後面加上字母 r，如果針對女性就加上字母 m：也就是說，這些逃脫的美洲奴隸的後裔不是簡單地說 Yes，而是說 Yesr（給男性），或者說 Yesm（給女性）。

這聽起來雖然荒謬，但我可以向你保證，旅行家或傳教士首次闡述的野蠻部落方言的描述，甚至更加離奇。不過，讓我們現在考慮一下，如果諸如 Yes'r 和 Yes'm 這樣的形式首次被語言學者注意到，他會怎樣做。他首先必須盡可能地從歷史角度將這些形式追溯至它們更為原始的類型，倘若他發現了它們與 Yes Sir 和 Yes Mdm 的關聯，他就會指出這樣的縮約形式最有可能如何在粗俗方言中產生。在將自由非洲黑人的 Yes'r 和 Yes'm 追溯至他們前美國主人的習語之後，語源學家接著會詢問諸如 Yes Sir 和 Yes Madam 這樣的短語如何開始在美洲大陸被使用的。

若在美洲原住民的方言中找不到任何類似的東西，他單憑詞彙的比較，就會被引導至歐洲的語言，並且在此處再次首先指向英格蘭的語言。即使沒有歷史文獻得以保存，語言的文獻本身也會表明，那些白人統治者——自由非洲人的祖先在奴役期間所採用的語言即是此等統治者的語言——最初來自英格蘭，而且在一定的範圍內，甚至可能確定英語最初被移植至美洲的時間。那種語言必定至少已經經歷了喬叟時代，才得以遷往新大陸。蓋喬叟有兩個肯定詞粒（affirmative particles），即 Yea 與 Figs，而他在兩者之間做出區分。

他僅在回答否定疑問時使用 Yes。例如，在回答「他不去嗎？」(Does he not go?) 時，他會說 Yes。在所有其他情況下，喬叟使用 Yea。對於「他去嗎？」(Does he go?) 這樣的疑問，他會回答 Yea。他在 No 與 Nay 之間也遵循同樣的區分，前者用於否定疑問之後，後者用於所有其他疑問之後。這種區分在湯瑪斯‧莫爾爵士之後不久即已廢棄，而且必然在「Yes Sir」和「Yes Madam」這類片語被定型化之前就已廢棄了。

但是從這些短語中還能獲得更多的歷史訊息。單詞「Yes」是盎格魯－撒克遜語，與德語的「Ja」相同，因此它揭露了一個事實：在喬叟時代之後越過大西洋的美洲奴隸的白人主人，在更早的時期就已經越過英吉利海峽，而那是在他們離開盎格魯人與撒克遜人位於歐陸的祖國之後的事。「Sir」與「Madam」這兩個詞告訴我們更多的訊息。它們是諾曼語詞彙，只有可能是由諾曼征服者強加於不列顛的盎格魯－撒克遜人身上的。它們還告訴我們不止於此。因為這些諾曼人或北歐人原本使用的是日耳曼方言，與盎格魯－撒克遜語密切相關，在那種方言中絕不可能產生出「Sir」與「Madam」這樣的詞彙。因此我們可以得出結論：在諾曼征服之前，這些日耳曼人的北歐人必定曾在某個羅馬行省停留了相當長的時間，以至於他們遺忘了自己的語言，而採用了羅馬行省人民的語言。

我們現在可以追溯諾曼人的「Madam」回到「YES R AND YES M」235 及法語的「Madame」，並在其中認識到對拉丁文「Mea domina」（我的女主人）的訛變。「Domina」變為「domna」、「donna」及「dame」，同一詞彙「Dame」也被用作陽性詞，指「主人」之義，乃「Domino」、「Domno」及「Donno」的訛變。在主教之下統治為教會領主的俗世領主被稱為「vidame」，如夏特爾的「Vidame」等。法語感歎詞「Dame!」與英語中相似的感歎詞無關，而單純表示「天哪！」之義。老法語中的「Dame-Dieu」即「主上帝」。* 「Domina」（女主人）的派生詞是「dominicella」，後來演變為「Demoiselle」及「Damsel」。陽性的「Dame」（指「Domino」，即主人）後來被拉丁文的「Senior」所取代，而「Senior」是德文「elder」（年長者）的翻譯。這個詞彙「elder」本為榮譽頭銜，我們仍在「alderman」及原本相同的英語「earl」（北歐語的「Jarl」）中保留著它，這是類似於古英語「ealdor」的比較級。這個頭銜「Senior」原來表示「年長者」之義，只是很少被用來作為貴婦人的榮譽頭銜。「Senior」變為「Seigneur」，「Seigneur」變為「Sieur」，而「Sieur」很快就簡化為「Sir」。
因此我們看到，在如 Yesr 和 Yesm 這樣的兩個簡短短語中，可能蘊含著歷史的長篇章節。「Ja dame Dieus non vuelha / Qu'en ma colpa sia'l departimens.」（願上帝永遠不允許 / 分離落在我的罪責上。）
（古法語）「上帝藉由婦女施行大奇蹟」（*Grandes miracles fit dames Dex par*，《加林之歌》，杜康日版，第二卷，第16、19欄）。——雷努瓦爾，《詞彙集》，見 Don 條。在古葡萄牙語中，迪茲提到 *senhor rainha*、*mia sennor formosa*（我美麗的女主人）。
閱讀。倘若如同中國秦始皇帝（西元前二一三年）時期所發生的那樣，發生了一場普遍的圖書毀滅，掃除了所有歷史文獻，那麼即使語言處於最為敗壞的狀態，仍將保存過去的祕密，並將向後代子孫訴說其祖先從東方遷徙至西印度群島的故里與遷移軌跡。

乍看之下，在雅利安遷徙的兩個極端發現同樣的名稱——東印度群島與西印度群島——似乎令人驚訝；然而這些名稱本身充滿了歷史意義。它們向我們訴說了日耳曼民族——雅利安家族中最富活力與進取精神的成員——如何在其環球遷徙中，給予他們想像中就是印度本身的國家取名為西印度群島；他們如何發現了自己的錯誤，隨後在東印度群島與西印度群島之間做出區分；他們如何在西方建立了新的國家，並使東方衰落的王國重獲生機；他們如何傳播基督教，最終又如何通過廢除西印度群島地主的身體奴隸制，以及婆羅門教靈魂持有者的奴隸制，來實踐基督教精神，直至最終問候了雅利安家族當初啟程探索世界之際出發的故鄉。所有這一切，乃至更多，都可以在語言的龐大檔案中讀出。印度這個名稱本身便有一段故事要講述，因為印度並非本土名稱。我們從羅馬人那裡得到它，羅馬人從希臘人那裡得到它，希臘人從波斯人那裡得到它。為什麼從波斯人呢？因為唯有在波斯語中，首音 s 才會變為 h，而這個首音 h 又像往例一樣在希臘語中被省略。唯有在波斯語中，辛都河 (sindhu 是梵文河流之名) 或七條辛都河的國家，才能被稱作欣迪亞 (Hindia) 或印度 (India)，而不是辛迪亞 (Sindia)。若非祆教信徒將每個 s 都念作 h，我們永遠不會聽聞西印度群島！

我們因此藉由一個假設性的例子看到了在語言成長過程中必須有所準備的事物，現在我們更好地理解了為什麼比較文法學必須樹立一項基本原則，那就是看待語言中的任何事物都不能僅視為形式層面，除非已經盡一切努力將語言的形式要素追溯回其原始的、實質的原型。我們習慣於文法詞尾修飾詞義的概念。但詞只能被詞所修飾；雖然在我們科學的現階段，說所有文法詞尾都已被追溯回原始的獨立詞彙是言之過早，但即使在僅剩單一字母的情況下，仍有許多已經被追溯回來，足以讓我們確立一條規則，即語言的所有形式要素最初都是實質的。假如英語在《農夫皮爾斯》（Piers Ploughman）時代之前從未被記錄下來，我們將如何理解像 nadistou* 這樣的形式，而不是 ne hadst thou？Ne rechi，而不是 I reck not？在多塞特郡，Al ffm 就是「all of them」。I midden 是「I may not」；I cooden 是「I could not」。然而，梵語在被記錄成文字之前所經歷的變化，遠比我們在這些方言中所見的要深遠得多。在盎格魯‧撒克遜語中，我們發現 not 代替 ne wot（我不知道）；nist 代替「he did not know」（他不知道）；nisten 代替「they did not know」（他們不知道）；nolde。

現在讓我們來看現代古典語言，例如法語和義大利語。大多數的語法詞尾與拉丁語相同，只是因為語音變化而改變。因此 faime 是 ego amo；tu aimes 是 tu amas；il dime 是 ille amat。法語中 il aime 原本最後有一個 t，在 aime-t-il? 這樣的短語中又重新出現了。因此，法語未完成過去式對應於拉丁語未完成過去式，Parfait defini 對應於拉丁語完成過去式。但法語的未來式呢？amabo 和 aimerai 之間沒有相似性。這裡我們看到了一種新的語法形式，可以說是在人類記憶範圍內，或者至少是在歷史的光天化日之下，萌生的。那麼，rai 這個詞尾是像春天的花朵一樣綻放出來的呢？還是有些聰慧的人聚集在一起，發明了這個新詞尾，並誓言要用它來取代舊的 bo 詞尾呢？當然不是。我們首先看到，在所有羅曼語族語言中，未來式的詞尾都與助動詞「有」(to have) 相同。在法語中你會發現——

j'ai 和 je chanter-ai 我們有 (nous avons) 和我們將唱歌 (nous chanterons) 你有 (tu as) 「你將唱歌」(tu chanter-as) 你們有 (vous avez) 「你們將唱歌」(vous chanterez) 他有 (il a) 「他將唱歌」(il chanter-a) 他們有 (ils ont) 「他們將唱歌」(ils chanteront) 但除此之外，我們實際上在西班牙語和普羅旺斯語中發現，未來式的表觀詞尾被用作獨立的詞，並且尚未與不定式結合在一起。在西班牙語中，我們發現代替「lo hare」（我將做它）的更原始形式是 hacer lo he，即：
我注意到提供的英文原文片段似乎不完整，並且內容為古英文否定形式的例舉。根據您的翻譯規則，我應按學術散文語氣忠實翻譯。以下為繁體中文翻譯： 「noldest，因為我不願意、你不願意；nyle 為我將不；naebbe 為我沒有；naefth 為他沒有；naeron 為他們不曾是，等等。」
我有（*habeo*）。我們在普羅旺斯語中發現 *dir vos ai* 代替 *je vous dirai*；*dir vos em* 代替 *nous vous dirons*。因此毫無疑問，羅曼語族的未來式原本是助動詞「有」（to have）與不定式的複合形式；「我必須說」（*I have to say*）輕易地轉變為「我將說」（*I shall say*）的含意。

在這裡，我們清楚看到語法形式是如何產生的。一個法國人把他的未來式看作純粹的語法形式。除非他是學者，否則他不知道他的未來式詞尾與助動詞 avoir 是相同的。羅馬人根本沒有懷疑 amabo 是複合詞；但可以證明它包含一個助動詞，就如同法語未來式一樣清晰。拉丁文的未來式是透過音韻損變而消亡的。當末尾字母失去清晰的發音時，就不可能將未完成式 amabam 與未來式 amabo 分開。未來式後來透過方言更新而被取代，因為在拉丁文中使用 habeo 與不定式的現象被發現，例如在 habeo dicere（我必須說）這類表達式中，會逐漸融合成「我將說」。*事實上，無論我們看向何處，都看到未來式是透過複合而表達的。我們在英文中有 I shall 與 thou wilt，原本分別意為「我被束縛」與「你打算」。在德文中我們使用 werden，源自哥德語的 vairthan，原本意為「去」、「轉向」。在現代希臘文中我們發現 thelo（我願意），在 thelo dosei 中意為「我將給予」。在羅曼什語中我們遇到 vegnir（來臨），形成未來式 veng a vegnir（我將來臨）；而在法語中 je viens de dire（我剛才說）等同於「我已經」。

剛才所說的。法文的「vais dire」幾乎是一個未來式，雖然其原始形式是 vado dicere（我去說）。多塞特郡方言「I be gwain to goo a-picken stuones」（我要去撿石頭）是另一個例子。在拉丁文 amabo 中的 bo 毫無疑問地來自古老的助動詞 bhii（變成），在希臘文未來式 o-a> 中，則來自古老的助動詞 as（是）。我們現在再往回退一步，提出我們之前多次提過的問題：一個單純的字尾如何能產生如此重大的變化，使「我愛」（I love）變成「我愛了」（I loved）？由於我們同時已經瞭解到英文源自盎格魯‧薩克遜語，並與大陸日耳曼語和哥德語有密切關聯，我們立即查看哥德語的未完成過去式，以看看它是否保留了原始複合結構的任何痕跡；因為根據我們在先前案例中所見，我們毫無疑問地準備好在此處也發現某個助動詞用於在希臘文中形成特定的未來式。它有多種含義，但這些含義都可以追溯到梵文 man（manyate）—「思考」。就如同 anya（其他）變成 ἄλλος，manye（我思考）變成 μέλλω。*伊利亞特* ii. 39：「他仍然想要給特洛伊人和希臘人加上痛苦。」*伊利亞特* xxiii. 544：「你想你會從我手中奪走獎品。」*奧德賽* xiii. 293：「你不是想要停止嗎？」即你不是打算停止嗎？或者在這樣的片語中，如 *伊利亞特* ii. 36，「這些事不是注定要完成的」—字面上，這些事不是注定要完成。因此 μέλλω 被用於那些可能發生的事，彷彿這些事本身有意或打算發生或不發生；隨著原始含義被遺忘，μέλλω 遂成為一個單純的助動詞，表達機率。μέλλω 和 μέλλομαι 在「猶豫」的意義上，同樣可以由梵文 man（思考或考慮）來解釋。在古北歐語中，未來式同樣由 mun（意為）形成。
我愛。241 語法詞尾不過是獨立詞語的遺跡而已。

我們現在來看盎格魯-撒克遜語中的助動詞 do。

單數 複數 dide didon didest didon dide didon 如果我們只有盎格魯‧撒克遜語的過去式 nerede 和盎格魯‧撒克遜語的 dide，那麼 nerede 中的 de 與 dide 的同一性就不會很明顯。但在這裡你將會領會到哥特語相較於所有其他日耳曼方言所具有的優勢，無論是為了文法比較還是分析的目的。正是在哥特語中，且僅在哥特語的複數形式中，完整形式才得以保留。 R 242 我所愛的。

輔助動詞 dedujp、dedun 得以保留。哥特語單數形式 nasida、nasides、nasida 代替了 nasideda、nasidedes、nasideda。同樣的縮約現象也發生在盎格魯‧撒克遜語中，不僅出現在單數，複數形式也是如此。然而，由於哥特語與盎格魯‧撒克遜語之間的相似性如此之大，我們不能懷疑它們的過去式是按照同樣的規律形成的。倘若歸納推理有其真理性，那麼必然存在過一個原始盎格魯‧撒克遜語過去式*——

單數 複數 ner-e-dide ner-e-didon ner-e-didest ner-e-didon ner-e-dide ner-e-didon 而當 ner-e-dide 逐漸縮減為 nerede 時，nerede 在現代英語中就會變成 nered。因此過去式中的 d——它將「I love」變為「I loved」——本來是助動詞「to do」，而「I loved」就等同於「I love did」或「I did love」。在英語方言中，例如在多塞特方言中，每個過去式若表達持續或重複的動作，就由「I did」構成，藉此在「he died yesterday」和「the folk did die by scores」之間建立了區別；儘管從源頭來看，died 就等同於 die did。
不過，有人可能會很恰當地提問：「did」本身或盎格魯-撒克遜語的「dide」究竟是如何形成的，又是如何獲得過去式的意義的。在「dide」中，末尾的「de」並非詞尾變化，而是詞根本身，第一個音節「di」則是詞根的重疊形式。
巴恩斯，《多塞特郡方言》，第39頁。
243 事實是，所有古代過去式，或如通常所稱的強動詞，都是如同希臘文和梵文一樣，藉由重疊而形成的，重疊是根詞被賦予動詞性質的主要手段之一。*盎格魯‧撒克遜文的根詞 do 與希臘文 tithemi 中的根詞 the 相同，也與梵文根詞 dhā 在 dadhāmi 中相同。盎格魯‧撒克遜文的 dide 因此應該對應梵文的 dadhau（我放置）。」

現在，依此方式，雅利安語或印歐語族的整個或幾乎整個文法框架已被追溯至原始的獨立詞彙，甚至最初看似神祕的最細微變化，例如 foot 變為 feet，或 find 變為 found，也都得到了充分的解釋。這就是所謂的比較文法，即對語言所有形式要素進行科學分析，此分析以對雅利安語族眾多方言中同一形式所呈現的所有變體進行比較為前提。為此目的最重要的方言是梵文、希臘文、拉丁文和哥德文；但在許多情況下，波斯古語（Zend）、凱爾特方言或斯拉夫方言會介入其中，對那些在四種主要方言中都難以理解的形式投射出出人意料的亮光。波普（Bopp）所著《雅利安語比較文法》這樣一部著作的成果可以用寥寥數語總結。整個文法框架——派生、變格和動詞時態變化的諸要素——在雅利安語族分裂之前就已經確立。因此，梵文、希臘文、拉丁文、哥德文及其他語言中文法的廣泛輪廓，實際上是

相同；而明顯的差異可由音韻蛻變來解釋，這種蛻變受制於各民族的音韻特性。總的來說，所有雅利安語言的歷史不過是一個逐漸衰落的過程。當所有這些語言的語法詞尾都被追溯到其最原始的形式後，在許多情況下，便有可能確定其原始含義。然而，這只能通過歸納法來完成；而那個時期——如同在普羅旺斯語 *dir vos ai* 中一樣，古代雅利安語法的組成部分在雅利安人的語言和思想中保持著各自獨立存在的時期——早已在梵語成為梵語或希臘語成為希臘語之前就已經結束了。我們對於曾存在過這樣一個時期，可以毫無疑問地確信，就如同我們對於在煤礦層形成之前存在著蕨類森林的現實性毫無疑問一樣。我們甚至可以做得更多。假設我們沒有拉丁文的遺跡；假設我們根本不知道羅馬和拉丁文的存在；我們仍然可以根據六種羅曼方言的證據證明，必然存在過一個時期，當時這些方言構成了某個小定居點的語言；而且，通過蒐集所有這些方言共有的詞彙，我們可以在一定程度上重建原始語言，並據此勾勒出這些共同詞彙所反映的文明狀態。如果我們比較梵語、希臘語、拉丁語、哥特語、凱爾特語和斯拉夫語，同樣的事情也可以做到。在所有語言中形式和含義幾乎完全相同的詞彙，必定存在於後來形成雅利安族系主要民族的那些民族分離之前；而且，如果經過仔細詮釋，這些詞彙也將成為證據，足以說明雅利安人離開其共同家園之前所達到的文明狀態。可以通過語言的證據來證明，在分離之前，雅利安人過著農業遊牧民的生活——一種如同塔西陀所描述的古代日耳曼人的生活方式。他們懂得耕作、修築道路、造船、編織和縫製、建造房屋的技藝；他們至少能夠數到一百。他們已經馴化了最重要的動物——牛、馬、羊、狗；他們熟悉最有用的金屬，並配備有鐵斧頭，無論是用於和平還是戰爭目的。他們認識到血緣和婚姻的紐帶；他們跟隨他們的領導者和國王，是非之別由法律和風俗來確定。他們對神聖存在的觀念根深蒂固，並以各種名稱來祈禱它。正如我所說，所有這一切都可以通過語言的證據來證明。因為如果你發現像希臘語、拉丁語、哥特語、凱爾特語或斯拉夫語這樣的語言——在其初次分離後與梵語的接觸很少——具有梵語中存在的同一個詞，例如表示鐵的詞，這就是絕對的證明，表明鐵在雅利安人分離之前就已為人所知。現在，在哥特語中鐵是 *ais*，在梵語中是 *ayas*，這個詞既不可能被印度人從日耳曼人那裡借用，也不可能被日耳曼人從印度人那裡借用，因此必定存在於他們分離之前。除非在這些方言分離之前房屋就已為人所知，否則我們不可能在梵語、希臘語、拉丁語、斯拉夫語和凱爾特語中發現相同的房屋名稱。就這樣，一部雅利安文明史已經被編寫了。

已被寫入語言的檔案之中，時代遠遠超越任何文獻史料所能觸及的範圍。*「雅利安」(Arya)這個名字本身即屬於這段歷史，而我將在本次演講的剩餘時間裡，致力於追溯這個古老詞彙的起源及其逐步傳播的歷程。我原本打算在今日的演講中納入一篇簡短的比較神話學概述，這是我們學科的一個分支，它通過與比較文法復原詞尾的原始形式和意義相同的方式，來復原衰落詞彙的原始形式和意義。然而我的時間有限；而且，鑑於有人多次問我為何將「雅利安」(Aryan)這個名稱應用於我們剛才考察過的那個語族，我感到有責任給出一個答覆。

Arya 是一個梵文詞彙，在後期梵文中意為「高貴的」、「出身良好的」。然而，它原本是一個民族名稱，我們甚至可以在《摩奴法典》（Law-book of the Manavas）中看到這個詞彙的蹤跡，該典籍中印度仍被稱為 Arya-avarta，即雅利安人的居住地。在古代梵文中，在吠陀的讚美詩（hymns of the Veda）裡，Arya 頻繁出現為民族名稱及尊崇之名，涵蓋婆羅門諸神的祀奉者，與其敵人相對——這些敵人在吠陀中被稱為 Dasyus。因此，其中一位神靈因陀羅（Indra），在某些方面相當於希臘的宙斯（Zeus），被以下列言辭所祈求（Rig-Veda）：「願汝摧毀那些是 Dasyus 者；懲罰無法度者……」*見穆勒氏《比較神話學論文集》（M. M.'s Essay on Comparative Mythology），《牛津論文集》（Oxford Essays），1856 年。
如果雅利安‧布米（Arya-bhūmi）和雅利安‧德沙（Arya-deśa）以相同的意義被使用。
《阿維斯陀》247 將彼等交付予汝之僕人！願汝成為崇拜者之強大助手，我將在諸慶典中讚頌汝之所有偉業。

在吠陀時代後期的教條文獻中，雅利安（Arya）這一名稱被明確地限定於前三個種姓——婆羅門、剎帝利、吠舍——以區別於第四種姓或首陀羅。在《百道梵書》（Satapatha-Brahmana）中有明確的規定：「雅利安人僅指婆羅門、剎帝利和吠舍，因為他們被准許參加祭祀。他們不應與任何人交談，只應與婆羅門、剎帝利和吠舍交談。若他們不小心陷入與首陀羅的對話，應當對另一個人說，『請把這話告訴這個首陀羅。』這是法則。」
在《阿闥婆吠陀》（Atharva-veda）（四‧二〇‧四；十九‧六二‧一）中出現了這樣的表達：「洞察萬物，無論舒陀羅或雅利安」（seeing all things, whether Sudra or Arya），其中舒陀羅和雅利安被用以表達全體人類。

這個字 drya（d 長音）源自 arya（a 短音），而這個名稱 arya 在後期梵文中被用來指稱毘舍耶（Vaisya）或第三種姓的成員。*原本所謂的第三階級必定構成了婆羅門社會的絕大多數，因為凡非士兵或祭司者皆為毘舍耶。我們因此可以理解，一個原本施用於土地耕作者與居家者的名稱，如何隨著時間推移而成為所有雅利安人的通稱。f 為何*f 在其中一部吠陀中，短 a 的 arya 被如同 arya 一般使用，以與首陀羅（Sudra）相對。我們讀到（Vaj-San. xx. 17）：「凡我等在村落、在森林、在家中、在露天所犯之罪，無論對首陀羅、對雅利安人——汝乃我等之拯救者。」

住戶被稱為雅利安人這一問題，目前會讓我們走得太遠。我只能說明雅利安（Arya）這個詞的詞源義似乎是「耕耘或耕作者」，並且它與arare這個語根相關聯。雅利安人似乎是為了將自己與遊牧民族圖蘭語族相區別而為自己選擇了這個名字，後者的原始名稱Tura暗示了騎馬者的迅速。^ 在印度，正如我們所見，雅利安（Arya）作為民族名稱，在後來的時代漸漸遺落，只被保存在雅利安瓦爾塔（Aryavarta，雅利安人的居地）這一術語中。但它在從印度遷往西北方的瑣羅亞斯德教徒那裡被更忠實地保存下來，他們的宗教雖然只以片段形式保存至今，但被保存在《綬德－阿維斯塔》（Zend-avesta）中。現在，在綬德語中Airya意為可敬的，同時也是人民的名稱。* 在《吠尼代德》（Vendiddd）的第一章中，阿胡拉‧馬茲達向查拉圖斯特拉解釋他創造大地的順序，提到了十六個國家，每一個在被阿胡拉‧馬茲達創造時都是純淨而完美的；但每一個都依次被安格羅－邁紐斯或阿赫里曼所玷污。現在這些國家中的第一個被稱為艾瑞亞涅姆‧瓦耶約（Airyanem vaejo，雅利安種子），其位置據推測遠至貝盧爾塔格和穆斯塔格的西坡，靠近烏克蘇斯河和雅克薩爾特河的源頭，這是中亞最高的地帶。f 根據雅利安人自己的傳統，雅利安人從這個被稱為其種子的國家向南方和西方推進，在《綬德－阿維斯塔》中，雅利安人所占據的整個地區同樣被稱為艾瑞亞（Airyd）。一條 • Lassen, Ind. 當瑣羅亞斯德教向西傳播時，波斯、埃利邁斯和美迪亞都為自己爭奪雅利安人的名號。赫拉尼庫斯（他的著作早於希羅多德）已知亞利亞（Aria）為波斯的名稱。*希羅多德（七‧62）證實美迪亞人稱自己為阿里人（Arii）；即使對於美迪亞的最北部阿特羅帕特內，斯特凡努斯‧拜占庭人已為其保留了阿里亞尼亞（Ariania，非 Aria）這個名稱。關於埃利邁斯，其名稱據說源自艾拉瑪（Ailama），乃是艾瑞瑪（Airyama）的推測性訛變。^波斯人、美迪亞人、巴克特里亞人和索格底亞那人在斯特拉波時代甚至更晚的時期，說著幾乎相同的語言，我們因此可以充分理解，他們應該為自己主張一個共同的名號，以與圖蘭的敵對部族相抗衡。

那雅利安在波斯帝國是作為一個榮譽的頭銜而被使用的，這一點從大流士的楔形文字銘文中得到了清楚的證明。他稱自己為阿里亞（Ariya）和阿里亞─奇特拉（Ariya-chitra），即一個雅利安人及雅利安血統的人；而阿胡拉‧馬茲達（Ahuramazda），或如大流士所稱的奧拉馬茲達（Auramazda），在塔克內與波斯的圖蘭語族譯本中被對應翻譯，因此包括葉茲德與克爾曼，但排除了法爾斯。』——威爾遜，《古代雅利安》，第120頁。

AKYA. 2/51 比希斯頓銘文中「雅利安人的神」。許多波斯人的歷史名號皆含有同一元素。大流士的曾祖父在銘文中被稱為阿里亞拉姆那（希臘文Ariaramnes）（赫羅多德，vii. 90）。阿里奧巴贊尼斯（即尤厄格提斯）、阿里奧曼尼斯（即尤美尼斯）、阿里奧馬爾多斯，皆顯示同樣的起源。* 約在這些銘文同時期，亞里斯多德的學生歐德莫斯經大馬修引述，談及「祆教祭司與整個雅利安種族」，† 顯然使用「雅利安」一詞的方式與《齊德亞維斯塔》所言「阿里亞全地」相同。

當波斯在薩珊王朝的權杖下，經歷多年的外族入侵和佔領之後再度崛起，成為一個民族國家時，我們發現這些新的民族君主——祆教（Masdanes）的信奉者——在德薩西（De Sacy）破譯的銘文中，自稱為「雅利安人及非雅利安人種的君主」；用帕拉維語（Pehlevi）表示為 Iran va Anirán；用希臘語則為 Ἀριανῶν καὶ[ἀναρίων]。伊朗（Iran）作為波斯的現代名稱，至今仍保留著這個古老頭銜的記憶。
亞美尼亞的名稱中據稱也存在著相同的雅利安（Arya）元素。§ 亞美尼亞的名稱，即 aryajantu。《希羅多德》第一卷，101 節。

然而，此詞在琐罗亚斯德语（Zend）中並不出現，而在楔形文字銘文中用於亞美尼亞的名稱「阿爾米納」（Armina）的詞源也是可疑的。* 在亞美尼亞語中，ari 在最廣泛的意義上被用來指稱艾延人（Aiyan）或伊朗人（Iranian）；它也表示勇敢，特別是用於美地亞人（Medians）。† 因此，雖然 arya 一詞並未包含在亞美尼亞的名稱中，但可以證明它在亞美尼亞語言中確實存在過，作為一個民族性的、具有榮譽色彩的名稱。

在亞美尼亞西方、裡海邊境附近，我們發現了古代阿爾巴尼亞的名稱。亞美尼亞人稱阿爾巴尼亞人為阿格瓦人（Aghovan），由於亞美尼亞語中的 gh 代表 r 或 l，博雷推測在阿格瓦（Aghovan）這個名稱中也包含了雅利亞（Aria）的名稱。這似乎值得懷疑。但在高加索山谷中，我們遇到了說雅利安語言的雅利安種族——奧塞蒂人（Os of Osseihi），他們自稱為盧翁人（Lron）。沿著裡海及其流經的地區…第31頁。安克蒂爾無權將閃語族的 airyaman 當作亞美尼亞。
解經家將《耶利米書》中的米尼（Mini）譯作哈爾‧米尼（Har Mini），而由於同一地區被尼古勞斯‧大馬士革人（Nicolaus Damascenus）稱為米尼亞斯（Minyas），他推斷第一個音節乃是閃語族的哈爾（Har），意為山（見勞林森《詞彙表》條目）。
若拉森《印度古代史》第一卷第八頁註釋。亞美尼亞語中亦用 Arikh 作美迪亞人之名，約瑟夫‧穆勒已將之歸納為 Aryaka 作為美迪亞之名。《亞洲雜誌》1839年，第298頁。若如夸特雷梅爾所言，ari 與 anari 在亞美尼亞語中分別用以指稱美迪亞人與波斯人，此舉只能歸咎於誤解，且必為後出之說法。

253 在奧克蘇斯河與葉爾羌河之間，雅利安族與非雅利安族在數百年間混雜居住。儘管雅利安人與圖蘭人的關係充滿敵意，儘管他們之間不斷發生戰爭，正如我們從偉大的波斯史詩《沙赫納梅》所學到的一樣，卻並不能由此推斷出所有騷擾雅利安人定居地的遊牧民族都具有韃靼血統與語言。代表圖蘭人的圖爾瓦薩及其後代在印度晚期的史詩中被描述為遭詛咒且被剝奪了在印度的繼承權；但在《吠陀》中，圖爾瓦薩卻被描繪為膜拜雅利安諸神。即使在《沙赫納梅》中，波斯英雄也有背棄伊朗而投靠圖蘭人的，並率領他們對抗伊朗，正如柯里奧蘭努斯領導薩姆尼特人對抗羅馬一樣。由此我們得以理解，為何希臘作家提及的眾多圖蘭或斯基泰名字應當顯露出明顯的雅利安起源痕跡。Aspa 是馬的波斯語名稱，在斯基泰人名 Aspabota、Aspakara 與 Asparatlia* 中，我們幾乎不能不認出同一個語素。即使是托勒密將之置於斯基泰的阿斯帕西亞山脈名稱，也顯示出類似的起源。而且「雅利安」(Arya) 這個詞也並非只在奧克蘇斯河以西為人所知。有一個民族稱為 Ariacce，^另一個民族稱為 Antariani。^^ 大流士時代的斯基泰一位國王被稱為 Ariantes。薛西斯的同時代人以 Aripithes（即梵文 aryapati，瑣羅亞斯德語 airyapaiti）之名為人所知；而 Spargapithes 似乎與梵文 svargapati（天界之主）有所關聯。

我們因此將雅利安人的名稱從印度追溯至西方，從雅利安地（Aryavarta）到阿里阿那（Ariana）、波斯、米底亞，更為可疑地追溯至亞美尼亞和阿爾巴尼亞，以及高加索的伊隆人（Iron）和跨越烏滸河（Transoxiana）的某些遊牧民族。隨著我們朝向歐洲前進，這個名稱的蹤跡變得愈來愈淡薄，然而它們並未完全消失。

亞洲的雅利安人在向西遷徙時，面前開放了兩條道路。一條經過今日的霍拉桑*向北，穿過現在所謂的俄羅斯，進而到達黑海和色雷斯的沿岸。另一條從亞美尼亞出發，越過高加索或穿過黑海進入北希臘，沿著多瑙河進入德國。在前一條道路上，雅利安人在古代色雷斯的舊名「亞里亞」(Aria)中留下了他們遷徙的痕跡；在後一條道路上，我們在德國東部、維斯瓦河附近，發現了一個稱為「阿里人」(Arii)的日耳曼部族。正如我們在波斯發現許多以雅利安(Arya)為重要成分的人名一樣，我們在德國歷史上也再次發現了如「阿里歐維斯圖斯」(Ariovistus)這樣的人名。儘管我們在希臘人和羅馬人中徒然尋覓這個古老民族名號的任何痕跡，但有些學者相信它可能已被保存在雅利安人向西遷徙的最西端，即愛爾蘭這個名字本身中。關於愛琳(Erin)的通常詞源學解釋是，它意為「西方之島」(iar-innis)，或大流士的土地。
史蒂凡努斯‧拜占庭（Stephanus Byzantius）。

阿里亞（ARYA）。255 西方，iar-in。但這顯然是錯誤的。* 古老的名稱在主格中是 Eriu，後來是 Eire。只有在斜格中才出現最後的 n，如同拉丁文詞彙（如 regio, regionis）一樣。因此 Erin 被解釋為 Er 或 Eri 的衍生詞，據稱是愛爾蘭凱爾特人的古代名稱，保存在盎格魯-撒克遜人對其國家的命名 Ireland 中。^ O'Reilly 主張，儘管他人否認，er 在愛爾蘭語中的用法與梵文的 arya 一樣，具有「高貴」的意義。\ ne s'ecrit jamais er ou eir, et la forme larin ne se rencontre nulle part pour Erin.' Zeuss 給出 iar-rend，西方島嶼（insula occidentalis）。但 rend（正確為 rind）在單數屬格中作 rendo。
古北歐語 irar、愛爾蘭人，盎格魯-撒克遜語 ira、愛爾蘭人。
雖然我是根據皮克泰先生的權威來陳述這些觀點，但我認為應該加上以下註記，由一位傑出的愛爾蘭學者好心寄予我的：——

愛爾蘭的常見名稱在最古老的愛爾蘭手稿中為 **Éyeriu**（屬格 **Éierenn**、與格 **Éerinn**）。起首的 **h** 常被省略。在對該詞進行詞源考證之前，我們必須試圖確定其古凱爾特語的形式。在希臘文和拉丁文作家筆下所見的愛爾蘭古代名稱中，唯一能由 **éeriu** 正式代表的是 **Hibernio**。此形式的單數離格 **Hibernione** 出現在《阿瑪赫手稿》（*Book of Armagh*）中，該手稿為九世紀早期的拉丁文手稿。從同一手稿，我們還可得知愛爾蘭人民的一個名稱為 **Hyberionaces**，它明顯是衍生自 **Hibernio** 詞幹。 現在，如果我們回想古愛爾蘭抄寫員常在以元音開頭的詞前加上 **h**（例如 **h-abunde**、**h-arundo**、**h-erimus**、**h-ostium**），並且他們也時常用 **b** 代替輔音 **v**（例如 **bobes**、**fribulas**、**corbus**、**fabonius**）；如果我們還進一步注意到威爾士語和布列塔尼語中愛爾蘭的名稱——**Ywerddon**、**Iverdon**——指向一個以 **IVER** 開頭的古凱爾特語名稱，那麼我們就不難給出 **Hibernio** 一個正確的拉丁化形式，即 **Iverio**。在古凱爾特語中，此形式應為 **Iveriu**（屬格 **Iverionos**）。同樣，**Fronto** 的古凱爾特語形式為 **Frontu**，正如我們在第 256 頁所見。

在追溯雅利安家族的古代名稱時，這裡蒐集的某些證據可能顯得存疑，而我自己也曾指出，將印度最早的名稱與愛爾蘭的現代名稱串聯起來的某些環節，比其他部分較為薄弱。然而主要的環節是穩固的。國家、民族、河流和山脈的名稱具有非凡的生命力，它們將永久存在，而城市、王國和民族則會消亡。羅馬在舊普瓦捷（Vieux Poitiers）的高盧人銘文中具有同樣的名稱。由於當 v 被母音所夾時在愛爾蘭語中總是脫落，Iveriu 將變為 ieriu，然後，前兩個母音相連，成為 eriu。至於 eriu 斜格形式中的雙 n，屬格 erenn（例如）之於 Iverionos 就如同古愛爾蘭語 anmann「名稱」之於梵文 namani 和拉丁文 nomina。n 的雙音可能是由古凱爾特重音引起的。那麼 Iveriu 的詞源是什麼呢？我冒昧地認為，它可能（如同拉丁文 Aver-nus、希臘文 Ἀφορ-γος）與梵文 avara「後方」、「西方」相連。同樣地，愛爾蘭語 des、威爾斯語 deheu「右」、「南」就是梵文 dakshina「右側」，而愛爾蘭語 air（在 an-dir 中），如果它代表 pair「東」，就是梵文 purva「前方」。

M. 皮克特將托勒密的 'Ἱουερνία (Ivernia) 視為最接近該名稱的古凱爾特形式。他進一步在第一音節中看到他所謂的愛爾蘭語 ibh「土地」，不僅與吠陀語的 ibha「家族」相通，也與古高地日耳曼語的 eiba「地區」相通。但首先，根據愛爾蘭語音韻規律，ibha 在古愛爾蘭語中應呈現為 eb，在現代愛爾蘭語中呈現為 eabh。其次，eiba 中的 ei 是雙元音 = 哥德語 diy、愛爾蘭語 oi、oe、梵語 e。因此 ibh 和 ibha 無法與 eiba 等同。第三，名詞單數主格中根本沒有 ibh 這樣的詞，儘管它出現在歐瑞利的字典中，伴隨著他對強化前綴 er— 的解釋，稱其為「高貴的」，以及許多其他錯誤和偽造。ibh 這個形式固然可以產生，但它不過是 ua「後代」的現代與格複數形。愛爾蘭地區經常以佔領該地的氏族名稱命名。這些氏族往往被稱為「某人的後代」(Awi, hi, «)。因此產生了愛爾蘭詞典編纂者的這一錯誤。——W. S.

ARYA. 257 至今仍保有這個名稱，並且很可能永遠保有，這是由最早的拉丁人和薩賓人定居者賦予的；無論我們在何處發現羅馬這個名稱——無論是在瓦拉幾亞（其居民稱之為盧馬尼亞）、或在格勞賓登州的方言羅曼什語中、或在羅曼語族的名稱裡、或在阿拉伯人給予希臘人的 Rouma 這個名稱中，以及在盧美利亞的名稱裡——我們都知道某些線索會引領我們回到羅慕路斯和瑞摩斯的羅馬，拉提姆最早戰士的堡壘。上扎格河口附近現在通常稱為寧錄的殘破城市，被阿拉伯地理學家稱為 Athur，而在 Athur 中我們辨認出古代亞述的名稱，狄奧‧卡西烏斯將其寫作 Atyria，並且注意到蠻族人將西格瑪字改為陶字。亞述在大流士的銘文中被稱為 Athura。*我們聽聞在薩特萊奇河上進行的戰役，我們幾乎沒有想到錫克人的戰場幾乎就是亞歷山大與旁遮普諸王戰鬥的地方。但薩特萊奇河的名稱就是同一條河的名稱——亞歷山大的赫蘇德魯斯河、印度人的薩塔德魯河；而在最古老的吠陀讚美詩中（大約公元前 1500 年），我們發現了一首戰歌，記述了在同一溪流的兩岸進行的一場戰役。
無疑，僅憑名稱的相似性來立論存在著危險。格林可能說得對，塔西佗筆下的阿里人（Arii）原本是哈里人（Harii），其名稱與雅利安（Arya）無涉。但既然雙方的證據都僅屬推測性質，此問題必須保持開放。然而在大多數情況下，嚴格遵循每種語言特有的音韻規律，將能消除所有的不確定性。格林著，第258頁 AKYA。
在其《德語歷史》（第228頁）中，作者想像赫拉特在楔形文字銘文中的名稱哈里瓦（Hariva）與艾里（Arii）有關聯，而艾里正如我們所見，是希羅多德給予米底人的稱呼。這是不可能的，因為哈里瓦中的初始氣音指向一個在梵文中以s開頭的詞，而非如雅利安（Arya）般以元音開頭的詞。下列的評論將使此點更加明晰。

赫拉特（Herat）又稱赫里（Heri），其所在的河流亦稱赫里魯德河（Heri-rud）。托勒密稱這條赫里河為「阿里亞西斯」（'Apa.ota.Sii），其他作者稱之為阿里烏斯河（Arius）；亞里亞（Aria）則是介於西方帕提亞（Parthuwa）、北方馬吉亞那（Marghush）、東方巴克特里亞（Bakhtrish）和阿拉霍西亞（Harauwatish）、南方德蘭吉亞那（Zaraka）之間的國家名稱。然而，雖然此名不帶初始的A字，但並非斯特拉波所述之亞里安那（Ariana），而是其組成部分的獨立國家。據推測，此地應與楔形文字銘文中的哈雷瓦（Hariva）相同，但此說有待商榷。然而在《瑣羅亞斯德教聖典》（Zend-avesta）中則以哈里都（Haruyu）之名出現，被列為奧爾穆茲德（Ormuzd）所創造的第六個國家。我們甚至可以追溯這個帶有h初音的名稱……

據推測，《阿維斯陀》(Zend-avesta)中的 haroyum 代表 haraevem，其主格形式並非 Haroyu，而是 Haraevd。(Oppert, Journal Asiatique, 1851, p. 280.)儘管無須否認此觀點的正確性可能性──該觀點部分受到賓格 vidoyum(源自 vidaevo，即「提婆的敵人」)的支持──但仍無理由不將 Haroyum 視為 Haroyu 的規則賓格形式，其中賓格中的長 u 乃由於詞尾鼻音所致。(Burnouf, Yasna, Notes, p. 103.)此 Haroyu 在主格形式上如同梵文的 Sarayu 般規則，甚或更為規則，蓋 haroyu 將預設一梵文形式 sarasyu 或 saroyn，源自 saras。

AKYA. 259 超越了瑣羅亞斯德的時代。瑣羅亞斯德教徒是來自北方印度的一個殖民群體。他們曾經與那些神聖的歌謠被保存在吠陀中的民族在一起生活過一段時間。後來發生了分裂，瑣羅亞斯德教徒向西方遷移至阿剌喬西亞和波斯。在他們的遷徙過程中，他們所做的正如希臘人建立新的殖民地所做的那樣，正如美國人開闢新城市所做的那樣。他們把新的城市和安頓下來的河流以他們熟悉的、能夠讓他們想起離開的地方的那些城市和河流的名字來命名。現在，既然波斯語的 h 對應於梵文的 s，那麼 Haroyu 在梵文中就是 Saroyu。印度的神聖河流之一，在吠陀中被提及，並在史詩中因其作為阿約迪亞河而聞名——阿約迪亞是印度最早的首都之一，現代的阿瓦德或哈努曼-加爾希——其名為薩拉瑜河（Sarayu），現代的薩爾朱河。正如比較語言學已經這樣把雅利安人的古老名稱從印度追溯到歐洲，作為雅利安人在離開他們共同的家鄉之前所採用的原始名號，因此把它選為該語言族的技術術語就是很自然的事了——該語言族以前曾被稱為印歐語族、印度‧歐洲語族、高加索語族或閃姆語族。
薩若由（Sarayu）也出現帶長音 u 的形式；參見威爾遜條目。M. 奧珀特正確地將哈拉瓦地區的人民與《阿普洛特》相比附，而非如格林所為，與《阿普歐特》相比附。
saras（水）、sarit（河流）和 Sarayu（烏德首都附近河流的專有名詞）；我們可以以相當高的蓋然性推論，這個 Sarayu 或 Sarasyu 賦予了河流 Arius 或 Heri，以及「Apia」或赫拉特地區其名稱。無論如何，作為赫拉特名稱的「Apia」與雅利安人廣袤鄉土的「Apia」毫無關連。
